content:
Clarence and Marissa get more space on the page than Nina, and perhaps that’s because she’s younger and her concerns fall lower in the hierarchy, or because the narrative is meant to start from the perspective of the two concerned parents who can’t locate their daughter during a chaotic moment in time and since the parents can’t reach her than neither can the reader? As I posited that, I do realize that Marissa and Nina are on the phone together during the happening. Worth noting regardless, I reckon.

There is an exterior violence that Nina the young adult experiences and is quickly able to nearly trivialize (asking Clarence for a gun, but not that kinda gun) that matches the internal self-harm of the adults which they both seem so comfortable experiencing and then trivializing; how they recover and cope with life’s violence is what builds their character. Nina almost died and now she is the one who is most alive, and Marissa nearly commits career-suicide to reel herself back from a hazy dream to a more grounded reality in which she can tell what matters again leading to Marissa and Clarence’s swaying-and-making-ammends dance in the final lines; the reconciliation Clarence also seemed to yearn for.
context: From document: “A Stone More Precious” Notes — chunk 2 (chunk 2/3)
